segments.text: Hello and welcome to the Autodesk DevCon.
I am happy that so many of you are here today for our presentation
about automating reporting processes with APS.
For those of us working as a contractor, maybe also in the MEP sector like the
EGOT Technologies, probably all of us have the same problem.
We cannot create our own digital processes because our customers are
ready yet or have different processes in place.
And everyone here knows, on the construction site, there is still a lot to do
in the digital transformation. Every single day we handle hundreds of issues, forms, photos,
checklists and documents, created in a field, in the office or somewhere between.
Today we want to show you how you can still build your own digital processes
and how the APS APIs can support you.
About us. My name is Michael and I've been with the
your industries almost 29 years now. I started as a technical draftman and for many years,
I worked in the field directly on our projects.
Today I focus on BIM processes, workflows and the digital transformation of our company.
My job is to develop digital solutions so they work in practice.
I'm here today with my colleague Samuel. He has been part of my team almost three years now.
He develops our extensions and interfaces for Autodesk construction cloud.
His thoughts were standard platform functionality ads.
He builds APS powered solution to optimize our daily work and allow us to rethink our existing processes.
Both of us follow a simple principle. First, understand what they want to do.
Then simplify it and then automate.
To understand from which perspective we are talking today, a short overview about
EGRE industries. EGRE industries has four main units. The first and the most important for today
is EGRE technologies, which includes all companies that work in MAP and plant engineering sector.
All of these companies work as contractors. As contractors, we must follow the processes of our
customers, which differ from project to project. At the same time, the amount of information we have
to deliver keeps growing. Customers want more details, more information, more documentation
and all of these have to be made transparent.
The other units are EGRE construction, which includes our construction companies such like BOM,
EGRE development, which focus on project development with UBM and EGRE innovation,
which invests into startups. As you can see, EGRE industries covers all parts of the construction
life cycle. This allows us to deliver and to end solutions.
In 2024, EGRE technologies generated 720 million euros and employed about 3,500 people
across 44 locations in seven different European countries. All units of EGRE industries together
generate 8 billion euros with more than 25,000 employees.
First, we will start with a short overview of the requirements in our projects,
and by EGRE reporting was the first important step for us. Then, some will explain how we start
with low code and how we worked on our first solutions. After that, he will show the limits
we reached with low code. And finally, he will explain how we used raw code to scale our solutions
across many projects. Yes, welcome to the reality of MAP contractors, where every project runs
on a different tool. In most projects, we receive the contract very late in the process,
where a lot of details are already set. Therefore, we have no control on the format in which data
must be delivered. There are many different requirements depending on the projects. Sometimes,
we must deliver data printed on paper or PDFs. In other cases, we must use accl-sheets or external tools
like Lahnrader, Fieldwire or Dalux, just to name a few. In the past, we tried to adjust our work
to each project tool. As a result, we did not get real learnings for the next project.
At the same time, many team members worked in more projects parallel, each with complete,
completely different workflows and tools. So, we made a clear decision in 2022.
We must implement our own processes no matter which platform is used from the customer.
And we did not want simply copy our old analog workflows in the digital form,
we wanted to rethink and optimize them. In 2020, we prepared a full move from AutoCAD to
Ravit, including Cloud Worksharing and Autodesk stocks. So, it was the logical next step to
expand our use of the AutoDust Construction Cloud with Autodesk build.
The new workflows we create link different functions in AutoDust build together.
For example, forms linked with issues, issues linked with photos,
asset linked to forms, and sometimes even more modules combined in one workflow.
Let me give you a simple example with photo documentation.
On every project, thousands of photos are taken. Usually, ending up in random folders,
phones or chats, completely disconnected from the context where they were created.
We changed this fundamentally. For every room, on the side, we are to
medically create an issue. This issue is the central anchor point of the photo documentation
and is also placed on a sheet. This way, all project members can easily take photos with
the smartphone or tablet app and every single photo is always connected to the correct room.
So, we connect issues, photos, sheets, and locations in one workflow.
This means it's not just about reporting. It's about organizing all project data in a structured way,
so that it is always available when needed. And this is extremely important because it becomes
the foundation for all future interfaces and integrations with other systems.
Having our own workflows was helpful. We could work with the same functions and tools in every project,
but even with that, we could not deliver the information in the way our customers wanted.
So, we needed a solution that works for both sites.
We needed a reporting system that produced results immediately, not hours or days later.
Don't get me wrong. Power BI and the data connector are great tools, but for the daily work
of a project manager, they are simply too slow. In a word, when new information is added,
updated and connected to others all the time. In the field, in the office or directly in a meeting,
by anyone in the project team, we need information within seconds.
Let me show a short example.
In this video, we show how we handle the daily field report. A daily report contains static
information, how many workers were on the field, what activities were going on, and what work was done.
We create this information directly in build forms and they are filled out every day.
At the same time, a lot of dynamic information are created in the project, for example,
block problems, blockers or safety issues. These issues are created in build completely
independent from the form and they can be created by anyone in the project team.
I cannot simply write these problems into a form and hope something will happen. I need to
track them, document them, add photos and so on until they are full resolved.
But in the end, we still need to bring all of this information together in one clear report.
This is exactly what you see here in this video. A report that includes the static information
like weather, man-bowers and activities and also all open issues that are still active and need
to be solved. And with this, I would now like to hand over to my colleague Samuel,
who will explain more details about the technology behind.
What are we doing this? The answer is our own ego reporting platform.
The trial started about 4 years ago with roughly 10 project. Back then, it was one of the side
project and the over scope was still unknown. Our guiding principle was build fast and felt cheap.
So we started with the simple and very limited reports. In some cases, the date that was up to 8
hours old. Not ideal, but acceptable for a start. We knew this was far from perfect,
but we needed to start somewhere. At that time, we had no experience using Autodesk platform
services and still had to build up our knowledge. Luckily, there was some great tutorials and
solid documentation from Autodesk. We chose a low-code approach because it allowed us to get
results quickly in the chief early wins. We were able to integrate the new reports into our
existing reporting system. In the end, we needed almost no new infrastructure that helped us to
keep overhead and costs so minimum. Let's have a look at our journey. We started with just 10
projects, but within one year we had five times as many. By the end of 2023, we had surpassed 100
project mark and the reporting started to take off. Today, we served over 600 projects with our
reporting platform and the number continues to grow. Currently, ego reporting manages an
extensive and growing data set, including more than 100,000 issues, twice as many assets,
300,000 photos and approximately 1.4 million relationships between different entities.
In total, the system operates today on nearly 15 million individual records.
Let me show you two milestones. Three years ago, when we had around 50 projects,
we noticed a growing desire from our users for more complex reports. We saw the potential,
took the chance and decided to expand our reporting system.
Six months later, we reached the 100 project mark. At the same time, we were able to introduce
real-time updates for our reports, more reports and faster report loading for users.
How did we achieve this?
We used Power Automate to fetch the desired data from our desk construction cloud and
perform some initial data transformations. After that, we transferred the data to our secret
server for further processing. We might ask why we didn't do our processing in Power Automate.
Well, data transformation in Power Automate has its limitations and can enlarge data sets
can take quite some time. So from this start, we decided to perform only the first filtering
and transformation steps in Power Automate and then hand everything off to our secret server to do
the rest. In the end, the data was stored in the correct tables so that our reporting applications
could access it. The secret server not only provides the data for our on-view
porting application, which Michael already showed you, you can also access the data from Power BI
on Microsoft Excel. This allows our users to build their own casting dashboards and lists
with data directly from our desk construction cloud.
Let's have a look at Power Automate. We chose it because it integrates well in the Microsoft
ecosystem and offers great value, especially for the price. On the other hand, as with almost
low, with almost all low code platforms, there is no native ACC connector that meant we had to
build our own. But also few, one familiar with low code. A connector provides the different
actions that can be used inside workflows. In our case, we built our own APS connector based
on the official APS-REST API specification. The path to solution is more or less the same across
all low code platforms. Here's a trigger action that starts a workflow. For example, a carburetql
and incoming mail are a time-based trigger. After that, election blocks are executed sequentially,
passing data from one to the next until the workflow ends. While building workflows is quite simple,
building custom connectors requires the vanilla of the underlying APIs and technologies.
As our data warehouse, we decided to use SQL Server because we already had one in place for other
reports. So what did we learn using low code in the past years? First, we are glad that we chose
this path and we truly value the knowledge we gathered in the past years. On the bright side,
low code empowers more teams to build bigger projects. It is fast to get started and ship
solutions quickly. The cost of entry is quite low since there is little to no need for additional
infrastructure and no expensive staff training is required. There are also many integrations available
for your APS systems, document management systems, BI tools and many more. However, there are challenges
as well. The ease of use comes with a performance penalty. Depending on the use case, in our own
tests, pro code solutions run up to 10 times faster as the equivalent low code approach.
In the end, developers are still needed to build custom integrations, design complex workflows
and connect different systems. There are also a vendor lock-in effect.
Switching providers is difficult and therefore you are more vulnerable to pricing changes.
And finally, our platforms have used such limitations. There is strict and maximum scaling,
the number of actions and total executions of your workflows. This brings us right to the limits.
With more complex reports, with more data and the writing number of uses and projects,
we saw our first scaling issues in mid-24. This resulted in increasing load times as we
had multiple platform limitations. At first, we tried working around these limitations.
We optimized workflows and paralyzed as much as we could, but this was not sustainable.
At the time, we managed about 300 projects, less than half of what we have today.
You can only imagine what would have happened if we hadn't done anything.
To give you an idea of the scale, in just one year, we widen tripled our API calls to
Autodesk. Now hitting 116 million requests per quarter, only for reporting tool.
I know this number sounds abstract. To give you a better sense of what it means,
that the more than two API calls, every single second.
Therefore, in early 25, we decided that we needed a foundational change.
We decided to switch our backend to C-Shop.
Now, we are able to scale beyond the old platform limitations and a no longer vendor,
blocked and more flexible for the future.
Waiting, change from minutes to seconds. Some reports load now up to 10 times faster.
Additionally, we can reuse our business logic with only minimal adjustments,
as the chip provides us. This means we practically have no vendor login anymore.
And probably most important, the code can be reused across other projects as well.
However, building protocol solutions takes more time and to cross-mode know-how.
And, third-party integration is not as easy either.
How does our current text-tech look?
To fetch data from ACC, we now use serverless functions with NECShop.
We chose this approach because serverless functions are highly flexible and scalable.
Yet, still cheaper than first-scale server solutions.
There's no data transformation happening in the SQL Server anymore.
Everything is done directly inside the serverless functions.
Since the code is gradually generic, we can use this with providers and the no longer vendor log.
As our data here by our house, we still use SQL Server.
Simply because there was a reason to change it, and continues to scale without any issues.
How was the transition? The transition between the back-ends was smooth.
In fact, our users were completely unaware of the underlying changes,
except the reports became a lot faster.
How was it possible? The reporting applications for the users data same.
Our changes happened in the back-end.
After testing the new back-end, we removed both systems in parallel for a while
to be sure that everything worked as expected.
Then, we started switching models from the old 2D Neo system, one by one,
first issues, then forms and assets, and so on.
Whenever something didn't work, we were able to switch back to the old system within seconds.
To wrap it all up, in hindsight, we recommend low code at a starting point.
We get fast results and can achieve quick wins.
The overhead is comparatively low, and third-party integration is quite easy.
Most importantly, you quickly can experience with auto-desk platform services and expand the knowledge.
Nevertheless, once you need to scale up by an assertive threshold,
you won't get around using ProCode. It is faster and more efficient.
But it requires specialized knowledge.
Thank you for joining us today. We appreciate your time and interest,
and we hope that the insights we shared will help you build your own APS-powered solutions.
